questions.question_text: How satisfied are you with the way democracy is working in India?
sample_responses: B — Somewhat satisfied. Democracy in India is functional and has delivered on many fronts, but there are legitimate concerns about corruption, institutional independence, and economic inclusion that prevent me from saying 'very satisfied.' As a pragmatist, I appreciate what works while acknowledging what needs improvement.

Somewhat satisfied — B. Democracy is functioning at a basic level, elections are real, there is some accountability, but I have genuine concerns about how well it serves ordinary people like me. I'm not thrilled, but I'm not despairing either.

B — Somewhat satisfied. Democracy in India has real strengths: free elections, peaceful transfers of power, diversity of voices. But there are legitimate concerns about institutional quality, corruption, and whether ordinary people's voices truly shape policy. I'm pragmatic — I see both sides.

Somewhat satisfied — B. Democracy is functioning, elections are meaningful, and I do believe in the system. But there are real problems — corruption, inequality, the gap between what leaders promise and what actually reaches people like me. I'm not very satisfied, but I'm not completely dissatisfied either. It's working, just not as well as it should.

C — Not too satisfied. I believe in democracy as a system, I value my vote, but the reality for people like me — struggling to cover bills, losing savings to a medical crisis — tells me the democratic process has not delivered enough for ordinary people. Some things work, many things don't reach us. That's 'not too satisfied' — not angry rejection, but honest disappointment.

questions.question_text: Do you have a favorable or unfavorable opinion of Prime Minister Narendra Modi?
sample_responses: Somewhat favorable (B). Modi has achieved real things — digital infrastructure, welfare schemes reaching poor households, India's global positioning. But I have reservations about some economic decisions, handling of dissent, and certain policies that seemed more political than pragmatic. A 'somewhat favorable' captures my nuanced, case-by-case assessment.

B — Somewhat favorable. He has brought some stability and development focus, and there are programs that have reached ordinary people. But I have real concerns about prices, polarization in society, and some economic pressures on middle-class families like mine. On balance, somewhat favorable — not enthusiastically, but fairly.

B — Somewhat favorable. I acknowledge real achievements in governance and infrastructure, but the farm laws debacle, economic pain from demonetization, and concerns about polarization hold me back from being very favorable. As a moderate, I give credit where it's due but remain critical of failures.

B — Somewhat favorable. I appreciate his strong leadership, the national pride he projects, and some of his development work. But the economic pressures on ordinary families like mine give me pause. I can't say very favorable when I'm struggling with bills every month.

Somewhat favorable — B. I acknowledge some good works and schemes that have reached poor families, but I hold real reservations about the economic burden on households like mine. As a moderate I cannot say very favorable given my daily struggles, but I also see positives so I won't say unfavorable.

questions.question_text: Do you have a favorable or unfavorable opinion of the Bharatiya Janata Party (BJP)?
sample_responses: Somewhat favorable — B. I can acknowledge BJP's governance achievements while holding reservations about certain policies and their implementation. This reflects my case-by-case, pragmatic approach. I'm not a BJP loyalist, but I'm also not dismissive of what they've accomplished.

Somewhat unfavorable — not strongly against, but the concerns outweigh the positives for me personally right now. Economic pressures on my household, community concerns, and some policies I disagree with push me toward unfavorable, but I hold this lightly and could shift.

Somewhat unfavorable. As a Sikh moderate, I appreciate some of BJP's development work, but their strong Hindu nationalist identity and the communal tensions that sometimes follow make me uneasy. My faith community has complicated history with this political direction. I lean somewhat unfavorable but acknowledge it's not a simple picture.

Somewhat favorable. I appreciate strong leadership and some of the governance improvements, and BJP aligns with some of my cultural and religious values. But as a moderate who is struggling financially, I can't give them a very favorable rating when my day-to-day economic reality hasn't improved much.

Somewhat unfavorable. I am a political moderate and I try to be fair, but when I look honestly at my own life — the healthcare costs that devastated us, the economic pressure we face every month — I feel the big promises haven't reached people like me. I have some respect for the party's organizational strength and some of its welfare programs, but overall my experience leads me toward mild unfavorability rather than support.

questions.question_text: Do you have a favorable or unfavorable opinion of the Indian National Congress (INC / Congress party)?
sample_responses: C — Somewhat unfavorable. I respect the historical legacy and some policy positions, but the corruption record, weak economic stewardship in later UPA years, and dynastic leadership structure make me lean unfavorable overall. It's not a strong rejection — I'm open to specific Congress positions on issues — but as a party overall, I have concerns.

C — Somewhat unfavorable. Congress has a legacy I respect, but their recent performance, internal disorganization, and failure to connect with ordinary people's concerns leaves me skeptical. I'm not strongly against them, but I can't say I view them favorably right now.

Somewhat unfavorable. I respect that Congress has historical importance and has done some good things for the country, but their current state — the dynastic leadership questions, inability to present a coherent alternative vision, and performance gaps — leaves me disappointed. I'm not hostile, but I'm not impressed either.

Somewhat unfavorable. I'm not strongly against Congress — I'm a moderate and I reject extreme partisanship. But right now their performance, leadership credibility, and inability to connect with common people's economic concerns leaves me more unfavorable than favorable. I'm open to reconsidering if they show real leadership.

Somewhat unfavorable. Congress hasn't shown me they have concrete plans for families like mine. The corruption allegations, the dynasty focus, the loss of direction — it all adds up. But I wouldn't say very unfavorable because I recognize their historical contributions and I'm not a strong partisan. It's a pragmatic, case-by-case judgment.

questions.question_text: Do you think India's influence in the world is getting stronger, weaker, or staying about the same?
sample_responses: A — Getting stronger. The evidence from credible sources consistently points to India gaining geopolitical, economic, and soft power influence. I'm not saying this out of blind nationalism but because the data supports it.

A — Getting stronger. I'm pragmatic and I look at actual signals, not just pride. The diplomatic engagements, the economic trajectory, the tech sector growth, the geopolitical positioning between East and West — India is genuinely earning more influence on the world stage. I have reservations about some domestic issues, but on global influence specifically, the trend is upward.

A — Getting stronger. India's global diplomatic presence, economic growth trajectory, technological achievements, and strategic importance in geopolitics all point to increasing influence. I'm pragmatic about this — there are real domestic challenges — but on the world stage, India's voice is clearly louder than a decade ago.

A — Getting stronger. Despite daily struggles with money and prices, I can see that India's position in the world has genuinely improved. More countries are engaging with India, India has a louder voice in global forums, and our technology and space achievements have drawn international respect.

A — Getting stronger. Whatever my personal hardships, I do believe India's standing in the world has been growing. The space program, G20, international diplomacy — these are real signs. My struggles are real too, but the question is about world influence, and on that I feel cautiously optimistic.

questions.question_text: Having a representative democracy where citizens vote for representatives who then make laws and policies — how good or bad would this be for India as a way to govern the country?
sample_responses: Somewhat good. Representative democracy is the right framework for a country as diverse and complex as India — it's not perfect, and implementation has serious problems, but the alternative systems have worse track records. I wouldn't say 'very good' because I see real dysfunction and corruption in practice. But the system itself, with free elections and representative governance, is net positive.

B — Somewhat good. I believe in the democratic system for India, but I'm honest that it has real problems — corruption, vote-buying, politicians who don't deliver. So I can't say 'very good' without qualification. But the framework of elected representatives making laws is fundamentally sound for a country as diverse as India.

B — Somewhat good. Representative democracy is fundamentally a positive system for India given its diversity and size. It ensures accountability and gives citizens a voice. But I'm pragmatic — the system has serious problems: corruption, dynastic politics, vote-bank politics, slow policy implementation. It's not 'very good' in practice, even if the idea is sound. 'Somewhat good' reflects my realistic, case-by-case view.

I would say 'Somewhat good.' Representative democracy is fundamentally good for India — it gives voice, accountability, and stability. But I'm not saying 'very good' because in practice there are real problems: corruption, politicians who don't deliver, caste-based politics, money power influencing elections. So it's good in principle and mostly in practice, but with real flaws that I can't ignore.

Somewhat good. I believe in democracy and representative government — it gives ordinary people like me a voice. But I say 'somewhat' rather than 'very good' because I see how corruption, money power, and caste politics can distort the system. The idea is good, the practice has real problems. It's not perfect, but it's better than alternatives.

questions.question_text: Having a strong leader who does not have to bother with parliament or elections — how good or bad would this be for India as a way to govern the country?
sample_responses: Somewhat bad. I'm not going to say 'very bad' because I can see the theoretical appeal of decisive leadership without political gridlock — sometimes parliament does obstruct good policy. But the downside risk of removing elections and parliamentary oversight is too serious. It's a bad structural design even if the current leader seems trustworthy.

C — Somewhat bad. I can respect strong leadership, but removing parliament and elections goes too far. Accountability matters. Even leaders I support can make mistakes, and without elections, there's no correction mechanism.

C — Somewhat bad. I won't say 'very bad' because I can imagine emergency scenarios or contexts where strong executive action is needed, and I don't want to be purely reactive. But as a general governing model, bypassing parliament and elections is concerning and likely to produce poor outcomes over time.

I would say 'Somewhat bad.' I appreciate strong, decisive leaders — that's important. But removing parliament and elections entirely goes too far. Those institutions, imperfect as they are, give voice to common people and keep power in check. Without them, there's no recourse if things go wrong.

C — Somewhat bad. I'm a moderate and I can see why people want strong leadership — I'm not against decisive governance. But bypassing parliament AND elections goes too far. Elections are how even poor people like me have a voice. Without that, there's no way to remove someone who stops caring about us.

questions.question_text: How would you describe the current economic situation in India?
sample_responses: B — Somewhat good. I want to be balanced and data-driven here. India's economy is genuinely growing in certain sectors — infrastructure spending, digital economy, manufacturing push. But inflation, unemployment among youth, and the rural-urban divide are real concerns. A pragmatic moderate assessment: it's not bad, but it's not as good as the headlines suggest. 'Somewhat good' captures the mixed picture honestly.

C — Somewhat bad. I'm not saying things are terrible or that there's no growth at all. I see construction, I see some prosperity around me. But for ordinary households, inflation and the cost of living have made things harder. It's not very bad but it's definitely not good either. Somewhat bad feels honest.

B — Somewhat good. I acknowledge India has been growing, and there are positive signs in infrastructure and digital economy. But inflation, unemployment concerns, and income inequality temper my optimism. It's not very good, but calling it bad feels too harsh given the overall trajectory.

C — Somewhat bad. I'm a moderate, so I won't say 'very bad' because I do see some development happening, infrastructure improving here and there, and I don't want to be overly pessimistic. But 'somewhat bad' honestly reflects what I and most people around me are experiencing — cost of living pressure, uncertainty, wages not keeping up.

C — Somewhat bad. I do not want to be dramatic and say 'very bad' because I can see that some things are working — roads are better in some places, some people around me have more phones and small luxuries. But for people like me, for most ordinary families I know, it is difficult. The economy is somewhat bad for the common person even if it looks good on paper.

questions.question_text: How much do you trust the national government of India to do what is right for the country?
sample_responses: B — Somewhat. The government has delivered on some fronts, particularly in digital infrastructure and certain welfare schemes, but there are real concerns about economic management, price levels affecting everyday people like me, and inconsistent policy execution. A pragmatic, data-driven person doesn't give 'a lot' of trust without consistent evidence, but 'not much' would be unfairly dismissive of genuine progress.

B — Somewhat. The government does some things right, development happens, but there are too many gaps, too much that doesn't reach ordinary people, for me to say 'a lot.' I'm pragmatic about this — I give credit where it's due and criticize where it's not.
B — Somewhat. The government does some things right and deserves some trust, but not unconditional trust. I hold them accountable on a case-by-case basis.

B — Somewhat. The government gets some things right and I respect the idea of strong national leadership, but my daily financial difficulties and the mixed record on helping common people like me means I can't say 'a lot.' I'm a moderate — I take a case-by-case view.

B — Somewhat. I trust the government to sometimes do the right thing, but not consistently enough to say 'a lot.' My family's experience shows the system has gaps. But I am pragmatic — I don't reject the government entirely. 'Somewhat' reflects my honest, moderate position.

questions.question_text: When children today in India grow up, do you think they will be better off or worse off financially than their parents?
sample_responses: A — Better off. On a case-by-case, data-informed basis, India's economic trajectory, expanding digital infrastructure, growing middle class, and educational access point toward the next generation having more financial opportunities than their parents. It won't be uniform across all regions and social groups, but the overall trend is upward.

A — Better off. On balance, I believe children today in India will be financially better off than their parents. The trajectory of education, digital access, and economic growth gives me cautious optimism, even though challenges like inequality and cost of living are real concerns.

A — Better off. Despite real challenges like inequality and cost pressures, the overall trajectory of India's economy gives more opportunities to the next generation than my parents had, or I had. Education access, digital economy, global connections — these open doors. I'm cautiously optimistic.

A — Better off. Despite my own struggles, I believe in India's trajectory. More education access, more technology, more opportunities than my generation had. My children will have access to things I could not afford. It will be hard, but better.

B — Worse off. I lean toward worse off, though not with full conviction. My own experience shows how fragile financial stability is. Medical costs, inflation, competition for jobs — these pressures are growing. Yes there are opportunities, but the cost of living and the risks are also higher. A single health crisis can undo everything, just as it did for us. I worry the next generation inherits more debt, more expensive housing, and less stable employment even if they have more gadgets.

questions.question_text: How important is religion in your life?
sample_responses: A — Very important. Religion shapes my values, my daily routine, my major life decisions. It's not just cultural background noise; it actively guides how I live.
A
A — Very important. Religion is a core part of my identity and daily life, not just a Sunday habit or cultural label.

A — Very important. Religion shapes how I make decisions, how I raise my children, how I find peace during financial stress. When bills are tight and I feel the pressure of not having enough, my faith is what steadies me. There is no other answer.
A — Very important. Religion guides my decisions, gives me strength when money is short and hope feels far away, and connects me to something larger than my struggles.

questions.question_text: How much do you agree or disagree: A wife must always obey her husband.
sample_responses: I'll go with B — somewhat agree. I respect traditional family values and the role of the husband as a key decision-maker, but 'always obey' is too absolute. A wise husband consults his wife, and a good marriage is built on mutual understanding within that framework.

Somewhat disagree — C. I don't completely reject the idea that in many situations a wife might defer to her husband, and I hold traditional family values. But 'always obey' as an absolute is something I can't fully agree with. It leaves no room for partnership or a wife's own judgment.

C — Somewhat disagree. I respect traditional family structures and believe spouses should generally support each other's leadership roles, but 'always obey' is too absolute. Marriage requires mutual respect and discussion, not unconditional compliance.

I'll say B — Somewhat agree. I believe in a traditional family structure where the husband has a leading role, and a wife should generally respect and support that. But 'always obey' is too strong — there are times for discussion, disagreement, and mutual decision-making. I hold a moderate traditional view, not an extreme one.

Somewhat disagree. I respect traditional family roles and I believe in a husband's leadership in the household — that is part of my faith and culture. But 'always obey' leaves no room for a wife's judgment, wisdom, or necessity. Life is not so simple. I would say somewhat disagree — not completely, because I honor the spirit of partnership and respect, but I cannot say always.

questions.question_text: When jobs are scarce, men should have more right to a job than women. Do you agree or disagree?
sample_responses: C — Somewhat disagree. I won't go completely disagree because I do understand the social pressures and traditional family structures that inform this view, and I try not to be dismissive of cultural context. But on balance, merit should determine employment, not gender. Efficiency and fairness both point against giving men automatic priority.

C — Somewhat disagree. I'm not going to say completely disagree because I understand the social and economic realities that make some people think this way, and I hold nuanced views. But at the core, I don't think being a man gives you more right to work than being a woman.

C — Somewhat disagree. I don't fully embrace the idea that men categorically deserve jobs over women. That said, I understand the cultural context and don't want to be dismissive of those concerns entirely — hence 'somewhat' rather than 'completely' disagree. But practically and fairly, jobs should go to the most capable and those with genuine need, not just based on gender.

I somewhat disagree. I understand the cultural logic, but I don't think women should formally have fewer rights to employment. Jobs should go to those who are qualified and need them. I'm not going to completely disagree — I recognize the social reality — but I lean against this as a rule.

C — Somewhat disagree. I hold nuanced views. I understand the pressure families face and that some households depend on a male earner. But I cannot fully agree that women deserve less right to work. I somewhat disagree rather than completely disagree because I understand the complex social realities in my community, but my core view is that women deserve equal access to work.

questions.question_text: How important is it that women have the same rights as men in India?
sample_responses: A — Very important. Equal rights for women is something I believe in both pragmatically and as a matter of basic dignity. India benefits when half its population has full opportunities.
A — Very important. Equal rights for women is something I feel strongly about, even while holding nuanced views on what that looks like in practice culturally.
A — Very important. Equal rights for women is something I believe in genuinely, supported by both my faith tradition and my practical, pragmatic outlook on what makes communities thrive.

A — Very important. Equal rights for women is something I genuinely believe in. It aligns with my sense of fairness, my faith's emphasis on human dignity, and my practical desire for my family members to have full opportunities and protections under the law.
A — Very important. As a woman who has had to stand on her own through crisis, I cannot say anything less. Equal rights for women isn't a Western idea — it's a survival necessity. I have lived it.

questions.question_text: How much of a threat is global climate change to India?
sample_responses: A — Major threat. The evidence is clear that India faces significant climate risks: disrupted agriculture, water scarcity, extreme heat, coastal flooding. As a pragmatist who trusts expert data, I can't ignore what the numbers show. Even if I hold nuanced views on policy responses, the threat assessment itself is fairly unambiguous.

A — Major threat. Even with my nuanced, case-by-case thinking, the evidence I've seen locally and through credible news sources points to real and serious impacts on India — water scarcity, extreme heat, flooding. I can't call this minor.

A — Major threat. The evidence I see locally and in credible reporting consistently points to India being highly vulnerable — flooding, drought, extreme heat, impacts on agriculture and water. Even if I don't embrace every alarmist framing, the practical threats to India are substantial.

A — Major threat. India is particularly vulnerable — agriculture depends on monsoons, coastal cities face flooding risks, and extreme heat events are already killing people. I may hold nuanced views on many things, but on this, the evidence around me points clearly to a major threat.

A — Major threat. Even though I hold nuanced views and am not strongly partisan, the evidence around me is clear. Climate change is already affecting India's water supply, agriculture, extreme heat, and coastal flooding. For poor families like mine, these impacts are devastating because we have no buffer. I don't need to be a political activist to see this is a major threat.
